statusLine: EU fuel security is under strain with 19 of 27 countries showing critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite overall EU averages remaining above mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
While EU-wide averages remain above the mandatory 90-day minimum (petrol 110.1 days, diesel 100.6 days, jet fuel 95.1 days), the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities across member states. Romania faces the most severe situation with all three fuel types in critical status, holding just 33.2 days of petrol and 32.4 days of diesel reserves. Poland, Luxembourg, and several other countries also show critical shortfalls across multiple fuel categories.

Jet fuel reserves present the most widespread concern, with 17 of 27 countries holding critical or warning-level stocks. This includes major economies like Germany (64.7 days) and France (67.5 days), suggesting potential disruptions to air transport networks. Diesel reserves are also problematic, with 13 countries below safe levels, including Spain at just 68.6 days despite being a major economy.

The 39.7% surge in Brent crude prices since late February, now at $101.29 per barrel, reflects ongoing Middle East supply disruptions that have prompted several EU countries to tap strategic reserves. This price pressure, combined with the uneven distribution of reserves across member states, creates a complex security picture where aggregate EU numbers mask significant individual country vulnerabilities that could affect regional fuel markets and cross-border supply chains.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries have critical reserves in at least one fuel type, with Romania most severely affected across all categories
Jet fuel shows the widest vulnerabilities with 17 countries below safe levels, including major economies Germany and France
Diesel reserves are strained in 13 countries, with Spain holding just 68.6 days despite its economic significance
Brent crude has surged 39.7% since late February to $101.29/barrel, driving fuel price increases across the bloc
Eastern European countries Romania, Poland, and Luxembourg show the most severe multi-fuel shortfalls
